title_en: The Feeling of Becoming Less and Less of a Person
title_zh: 成为“人”这一身份日渐稀薄的感觉
rubric_en: In Ben Lerner’s new novel, technology divides us further from one another, and ourselves.
rubric_zh: 在本·勒纳的新小说里，技术让我们彼此之间更疏远，也让我们与自己更加分离。

paragraphs.en: Let’s hazard an assertion: On or about June 2007, human character changed. To be more exact—because the phrase human character now feels antique—we might say instead that the human sensorium changed. By this we don’t necessarily mean a sudden and definite alteration in how we perceive the world—in the forms, sources, and amount of information we absorb, and in how we conduct our relations with parents, children, spouses, partners, mentors, friends. Yet a transition was set in motion, differentiating life before the omnipresent smartphone and life after, and dating its onset to the birth of the iPhone seems apt.
paragraphs.zh: 我们不妨先大胆断言：大约在2007年6月左右，人类的性格发生了变化。更准确地说——因为“人类性格”这个说法现在听起来有些过时——我们也许应该说是人的感知系统发生了变化。这里并不一定意味着我们感知世界方式出现了某个突发且明确的改变，而是指我们吸收信息的形态、来源和数量，以及我们与父母、子女、配偶、伴侣、导师、朋友这些关系的处理方式都在变化。然而，一场转变已经启动，它区分了“随处可见智能手机”之前的生活与之后的生活，而且把这一转折点追溯到iPhone诞生时期似乎是恰当的。

paragraphs.en: The above is a loose homage to words that Virginia Woolf wrote slightly more than a century ago about the collapse of Victorian gender and class norms, a shift that she placed more arbitrarily, with half-ironic playfulness, in December 1910, a decade and a half before her essay appeared. In both cases, the mutation seems at once massive and slippery, glaring and subtle; the effects are phenomena without edges, pervasive yet noticeable only when caught by middle-distance memory. Few things are as hard to discern as what was different about the recent past.
paragraphs.zh: 上文是对弗吉尼亚·伍尔夫在一个多世纪前写下的话的宽泛致敬。她当时谈到的是维多利亚时代性别与阶级规范的崩解，她更为任性地、带着半分反讽的嬉戏，把这种转变放在1910年12月来描述，这比她的这篇随笔问世早了十五年。在这两种情况下，这种变异都显得既庞大又难以把握，既刺眼又微妙；其效果是没有边界的现象，无处不在，却只有在某种中距离的记忆里才会被捕捉到。很少有什么东西像近代过去那样难以辨认——到底那时究竟不同在哪里。

paragraphs.en: Capturing changes like these calls less for history than for the oldest of forms, fable. Not far into Ben Lerner’s Transcription, you realize that a fable is, in fact, what you have entered. His new novel may be set roughly two years ago, and in a highly specific and contemporary milieu, but it is also about a man in the middle of his life, away from home, signposts vanished and maps lost. Having slipped into a different world, he must now wonder where, if anywhere, he might feel at home. Only the reality-bending qualities of fable, perhaps, can evoke the threshold in human cognition that was crossed just a short while ago and convey the mystery of how or if we have remained ourselves on the other side of that change.
paragraphs.zh: 捕捉这种变化，与其说依赖历史，不如说更需要一种最古老的文体——寓言。读到本·勒纳《抄写》不久，你就会意识到，你其实已经进入了一个寓言世界。他的新小说大体设定在约两年前，处在一个高度具体又当代的语境中，但它同样是在讲一个中年男子：离乡在外，路标消失，地图遗失。这个人一旦滑入另一种世界，就不得不思考自己还有没有、又在哪里，能感到“在家”。或许，只有寓言那种扭曲现实的特质，才能描摹人类认知跨过的那道门槛，并传达这样一种谜团：在那次改变之后，我们是否仍然还是原来的自己，或者根本无法说清。

paragraphs.en: Transcription is the fourth novel by a writer who is not solely, or even primarily, a novelist. Lerner is also a poet and critic, and his novels are written as intermittent and experimental eruptions of engagement with some matter of urgent public concern. They fit within the quasi-biographical, reference-rich, largely plotless genre that the 2010s came to know as “autofiction.” Yet Lerner’s version, which has the feel of theory in a different mode, has tilted slightly toward a much older tradition, the novel of ideas. Each successive novel has become less indirect about its subject. His debut, Leaving the Atocha Station, published in 2011, is a comic, cerebral coming-of-age novel about a young poet in the era of the War on Terror. Pressing issues claim the foreground more overtly three years later in 10:04, in which Lerner evokes life in the shadow of climate change, and in The Topeka School, published in 2019. In that book, his characters confront the decline of American public speech—its degeneration into varieties of unreason and the proto-fascist violence that follows.
paragraphs.zh: 《抄写》是一本作家作品集中较新的小说；而这位作家并非单纯，甚至不以小说为主要身份。勒纳也是一位诗人和评论家，他的小说更像是对某种紧迫公共议题的间歇性、实验性介入。它们属于2010年代兴起的一类“反自传”体裁——这种体裁以准自传、满载典故、并且大体缺乏情节的写法而著称。然而勒纳的版本有着不同形态的理论气息，轻微地偏向一条更古老的传统：观念小说。每一部续作都更少地绕弯，反而更直接地直面主题。他的处女作《离开阿托查车站》于2011年问世，是一部关于恐怖主义战争时期年轻诗人的机智而理性的成长小说。三年后的《10:04》更明确地把现实议题推到前台，在这部作品中，勒纳勾勒了气候变化阴影下的生活；而在2019年出版的《托皮卡中学》（《The Topeka School》）中，他的角色们直面美国公共言辞的衰落——以及它退化为各种不合理形态、随后酝酿出的原始法西斯式暴力。

paragraphs.en: From the October 2019 issue: Ben Lerner, portraitist of talkative men
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2019年10月刊：本·勒纳，爱谈话男士的画像师

paragraphs.en: The novels’ topicality is leavened by a critic’s erudition and a poet’s facility for turning abstractions into images. The protagonists of these books partially resemble Lerner himself: a male writer from Kansas, the product of an intellectual family and an elite education, eventually a resident of New York City. These figures seem to offer an internal portrait of the author as well. He’s hyper-articulate and earnest, though prone to disconcerting fits of duplicity and rage. He’s also almost paralyzingly aware of being the beneficiary of a wealthy nation’s tangible and intangible resources, and aware that those resources aren’t sufficient for a life lived well. Taken together, Lerner’s first three novels have the contours of a trilogy, installments in a collective biography whose theme is the evolving disquiet, even anguish, of late-imperial America.
paragraphs.zh: 这些小说的现实关怀被评论家般的学养和诗人式的抽象意象化能力所调和。书中的主人公在某种程度上与勒纳本人相似：一位来自堪萨斯的男性作家，出身于知识分子家庭，受过精英教育，最终在纽约市定居。这些人物似乎也勾勒出作者的内在自画像。他语言能力强、热情真诚，却又时有令人不安的伪饰与愤怒发作。他几乎清醒到近乎瘫痪，深知自己是富裕国家有形与无形资源的受益者，却也清楚这些资源不足以支撑一个过得体面的生活。综合来看，勒纳前三部小说呈现出一部三部曲的轮廓，是一部群像式的传记体，主题是后帝国美国日益变化的不安，甚至是痛苦。

paragraphs.en: Transcription is and isn’t like these predecessors. Lerner’s alter ego has returned, unnamed here and now firmly middle-aged, and the terrain is still that of cosmopolitan, credentialed, coastal-urban white America, steadily more embattled and self-doubting. But the book, divided into three encounters—conversations, really—has an unusually stark quality that gives it the feel of a parable. The subject matter is also different. Lerner here turns to media theory, despite the risk that this might seem both in the clouds and mundane: He’s training his attention on the phones in our pockets, on something very small and yet boundless in its effects.
paragraphs.zh: 《转录》与这些前作既相似又不同。勒纳的化身又出现了，如今在此处不具名，且已是名符其实的中年男人；叙事地带仍是世界化、持有头衔、沿海城市中的白人美国，环境却变得更加多事、更自我怀疑。但这本书被分成三次“会面”——其实就是三场对话——具有异乎寻常的冷峻质感，因此带有寓言般的气息。主题也与众不同。在这里，勒纳转向了媒介理论，尽管这看上去既有点飘在空中，又十分世俗：他把注意力投向了我们口袋里的手机，这个体量很小却在影响上无边无际的事物。

paragraphs.en: Read: The unhappy literary families of the internet age
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：互联网时代那些不幸的文学家族

paragraphs.en: Here Lerner’s dual talents give him a unique purchase. The “internet novel” of recent years has tried very hard to sound just like the internet—to compete with, parody, or mimic the deadpan, knowing wit and quick cuts of online discourse. Lerner’s media theory is both more deliberately, novelistically banal and more freely lyrical. Transcription is indeed about those phones and their most literal, pedestrian appearances in our lives. At the same time, it is about how they have transformed the very guise of the world, which means that the novel is about change itself, about what living through epochal transition is like. Trying to explore this process is a project that lacks the dignity or sense of emergency associated with confronting climate disaster or authoritarianism. The experience is happening everywhere; it’s the fish’s theory of water. Helping us recognize the atmosphere we’re immersed in has always been Lerner’s talent.
paragraphs.zh: 在这里，勒纳的双重天赋让他拥有了独特的切入点。近几年，“网络小说”拼命想听起来像网络本身——要么竞争于、要么戏仿、要么模仿网络话语里那种冷峻的、见多识广的机锋和快速跳接的节奏。勒纳的媒体理论既更有意地趋于小说化的平淡，又更自由地抒情。《记录》确实写的是那些手机，以及它们在我们生活中最字面、最平常的出现；与此同时，它也写着它们如何改造了世界的“样貌”，这意味着这部小说写的是“变化”本身，是经历时代性转折时是什么感觉。试图去勾勒这一过程，本身并不具备在面对气候灾难或专制主义时那种庄重感或紧迫感。这个经验无处不在，像鱼对水的感觉；对我们所处氛围的识别一直是勒纳的长处。

paragraphs.en: Transcription opens with its protagonist on a train to Providence, Rhode Island. He’s en route to interview his former college mentor, Thomas, a renowned German media theorist and artist, for a magazine on the occasion of his 90th birthday. It is early 2024. Masking and rapid testing are still on his mind. While on the train, he texts with his wife about their daughter’s school anxiety; listens to a talk that his mentor gave half a century earlier; falls asleep and has a disturbing dream about trying to pick up his daughter at school and being turned away. Tugged at by both the young and the old, he starts to feel unmoored. At his hotel, preparing for the interview, he accidentally drops his phone—which he was counting on to record the interview—into a clogged sink. It goes “black mirror.”
paragraphs.zh: 《记录》开篇是一位主人公乘火车前往罗德岛普罗维登斯的场景。他正前往拜访并采访他的前大学导师托马斯，一位著名的德国媒体理论家与艺术家，此行也是为了纪念这位导师九十岁生日，在一份杂志上做报道。时间是2024年初，口罩和快速检测仍占据着他的心头。乘车途中，他与妻子发短信讨论女儿在学校的焦虑；听一段导师五十年前发表过的演讲；昏睡过去，并做了一个噩梦：他去学校接女儿，却被拒之门外。既被年轻人牵引，又被年长者牵动，他开始感到失衡。到了酒店，临近采访前，他不小心把手机——本来想用来记录采访——掉进了堵塞的洗手池里。它成了一面“黑镜”。

paragraphs.en: From the April 2016 issue: Nicholas Dames on the new fiction of solitude
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2016年4月刊：尼古拉斯·戴姆斯评《孤独》新小说

paragraphs.en: He has been sundered from the world, unable to locate an Apple Store where he could replace the phone, unable to call his mentor to apologize in advance for being late, unable to make a promised FaceTime call to his daughter. He’s as inaccessible as the dead. On his way to the interview, he experiences the shift as a transfiguring estrangement, “a withdrawal indistinguishable from mild intoxication, the landscape made strange, the stones stonier.” Unmediated by a screen, he is seized by an almost unbearably sharp feeling of presence, while he is also moving back into his own deviceless past, as if the Providence of his youth has summoned itself from the underworld. We have entered the miraculous, uncannily familiar, possibly treacherous land of fable. Nothing captures the force of technology better than an enchantment that removes it, however temporarily. But the scene is also comically ordinary; he is just a man who cannot open his Contacts.
paragraphs.zh: 他与世界被分离了：他找不到可以更换手机的苹果店；无法给导师打电话提前道歉自己会迟到；也无法给女儿打出约定的 FaceTime。此刻他和死人一样触不可及。前往采访途中，他将这场突变体验为一种可怖的陌生化——“一种几乎无法区分于轻度醉酒的退离，风景变得诡异，石头也更像石头”。不经过屏幕的中介，他被一种几乎无法承受的清晰在场感骤然抓住；与此同时，他也向着自己没有设备的过去倒退，仿佛童年时代的普罗维登斯从冥界被召回。我们进入了奇迹般地、诡异得令人熟悉又可能危险的寓言之地。没有它的魔力化解，似乎没有任何事物比这更能呈现技术的力量；而这幕景象又荒诞得很日常：他只是一个打不开联系人列表的人。

paragraphs.en: Defamiliarization, in Transcription, always swerves back into the familiar, into realist farce. When he arrives at Thomas’s house, an incoherent shame seizes him, and he cannot admit to his mentor that his phone is unusable. His frail interlocutor begins to speak—elegantly, passionately—about his own youth in Hitlerite Germany and the fascist radio speeches that form his earliest memories, but his words are not being captured. And as this unrecorded rhapsody progresses, dementia weaves its spell. Thomas begins to confuse his young mentee and interviewer with his own son, mixing fragments of family wounds and recriminations into his reverie, as if he’s forgotten that he’s being interviewed. Lerner’s protagonist plays along politely, anxiously. What else can he do? The interview will have to begin again tomorrow, with a new phone, in any case. He fails to confess that nothing has been recorded. Thomas’s sibylline, disorienting words slip into the void.
paragraphs.zh: 在《转录》中，陌生化总是又回到熟悉之处，回到现实主义闹剧。当他到达托马斯的家时，一阵难以言说的羞耻缠住了他，他无法向导师坦白自己的手机无法使用。这个虚弱的对话者便开始优雅而热烈地谈起自己在希特勒德国的青春，以及那些构成他最早记忆的法西斯电台演说；但他的言语没有被记录下来。就在这段未被记录的狂想继续发展时，失智症如同咒语般展开。托马斯开始把自己的年轻学徒和采访者和自己的儿子混淆，把家庭创伤与指责的碎片掺进他的沉思里，仿佛他已经忘记了自己正在接受采访。勒纳的主人公礼貌而焦急地顺着他的话往下走，除此之外他无能为力。采访无论如何都得改天重来，换一部新手机。于是他没有坦白：什么都没有被录下。托马斯那种预言式、令人迷失方向的言语便滑入虚空。

paragraphs.en: We later learn, indirectly, that the promised tomorrow never comes—that Thomas’s death has intervened, and the recorded interview never occurs. The nonevent is the void with which the novel’s following two sections contend. The protagonist, we find out, has tried to fill it by publishing his conversation with Thomas as if it were a verbatim last interview, and the revelation of this imposture shocks not only Thomas’s other acolytes—a “deepfake,” one calls it, made under “false pretenses”—but also Thomas’s son, Max, the protagonist’s close friend from college. Their anger makes clear the scale of the protagonist’s oedipal betrayal.
paragraphs.zh: 我们后来是间接得知，承诺的明天并未到来——托马斯的去世介入了，预定录制的采访从未发生。这个“未发生事件”就是小说后两章要与之搏斗的空洞。我们得知，主人公曾试图填补它，他把自己与托马斯的谈话发表成一篇几乎逐字逐句的“最后采访”，而这一伪装的揭露不仅震惊了托马斯其他追随者——有人称之为“深度伪造”，是在“虚假借口”下制作的——也震惊了托马斯的儿子马克斯，那是主人公在大学时期的挚友。两人的愤怒清楚地显露了主人公所犯下的那种俄狄浦斯式的背叛。

paragraphs.en: In the novel’s final section, other crossed signals and cross-generational misperceptions emerge. Max now speaks, almost entirely uninterrupted, about his own failed communication with his father in recent years. He describes Thomas’s near death from COVID in early 2020, during which a nurse held a smartphone to his ear so that Max could say a final goodbye of forgiveness and apology, a call that Thomas may or may not have heard. Max also recounts a later visit with his recovering father, in the course of which he surreptitiously recorded their conversation on a phone—another deception. All of this is framed by Max’s narration of his young daughter’s life-threatening eating disorder and the treatment that saved her: She was allowed to eat while glued to her iPad, watching the bizarre micro-genre of unboxing videos. Max relates all of this with a lucid matter-of-factness, because he is baffled by it.
paragraphs.zh: 在小说的最后一章，其他交错的信号和跨代误读也随之出现。如今的马克斯几乎不间断地讲述他近年与父亲沟通失败的全过程。他提到2020年初托马斯曾濒临死亡，期间一位护士把智能手机贴在他耳边，让马克斯可以说出最终的道别：请求原谅并道歉，这通电话托马斯或许听见，也或许没听见。马克斯还回忆起一次稍后的探望：在父亲康复期间，他们有过一次会面，他悄悄用手机录下了那段谈话——又一次欺骗。所有这些都被马克斯放在一个框架里来叙述，那就是他年幼女儿患上危及生命的进食障碍，以及拯救她的治疗方式：她可以在紧贴着 iPad 的状态下进食，边看那种怪异的“开箱”短视频类型。马克斯把这一切娓娓道来，语气清醒而平实，因为他自己也被这一切弄糊涂了。

paragraphs.en: One might take Transcription’s final section as Max’s absolution—tacit, maybe even a bit grudging—of his friend, and his father’s mentee, for his transgression. Here the novel comes close to a kind of mythic resolution, one that lets us glimpse a deep, unappeasable desire: to have the dead, or the dead’s appointed representatives, forgive us, so that we may forgive them for having left us. Lerner, however, never lets us forget how entangled this need is with power cords and phones and screens, all the ganglia of modern, networked life—and how these devices are both bridge and barrier between parents, children, and grandchildren.
paragraphs.zh: 人们可能会把《转录》的最后一章理解为马克斯对朋友的——也就是对托马斯这位导师的门生身份的——一种默许、也许带着几分不情愿的赦免。小说在此逼近一种几乎神话般的终局，让我们看到一种深沉而难以平息的欲望：希望逝者，或逝者的指定代言者，能够原谅我们，好让我们也能原谅他们离开我们。然而勒纳却始终不让我们忘记，这种需求与权力线缆、手机和屏幕纠缠在一起——这些全是当代网络化生活的神经节——而这些装置既是父母与子女、孙辈之间的桥梁，也是他们之间的屏障。

paragraphs.en: As Lerner’s alter ego has aged through his four novels, he has acquired not just offspring but a profound unease about the different world that the next generation already inhabits, one that the parent not only can’t control but can’t even apprehend, as if the child’s receptive organs are attuned to as yet unnamed hues and higher frequencies. Meanwhile, the world of aging parents threatens to vanish unrecorded. In the remembered/fabricated interview, Thomas provides a media metaphor for this—a “split screen” in which the young and old exist in parallel spaces. “The question,” Thomas adds, “is how to depict the historical changes in atmosphere.” Transcription is an attempt at an answer.
paragraphs.zh: 在莱纳的化身通过其四部长篇小说成长为一个中年人之后，他不仅成了父亲，也对下一代已经生活在其中、却无力支配也难以真正理解的不同世界产生了深深的焦虑。那就像孩子的感官器官已经调谐到了尚未被命名的色调和更高的频率。与此同时，老去父母的世界正面临无声无息地消失。 在这段“记忆/伪造”访谈中，托马斯提供了一个媒体隐喻——一种“分屏”式结构，青年与老者并置于平行空间。“问题是，”托马斯补充道，“如何描绘氛围的历史性变化。”《转录》尝试给出一个答案。

paragraphs.en: Historical transitions have magically disorienting qualities. They also are a matter of prosaic, minute negotiations. Lerner’s characters always have thickly rendered intellectual lives, but in Transcription their intellection is directed, more than ever before, toward what you might call “getting used to it,” finding ways to acclimatize to that new atmosphere. To the communication-and-storage devices in their pockets that, far more than any postmodern philosophy, have erased the feeling of embodied, subjective presence in the world. To the anxiety disorders of their children, which have the portentous mystery of an oracle’s riddle. To the garbling, which the novel relays in its opening section, of collective social-historical memory.
paragraphs.zh: 历史性的转换带有近乎神奇的失位感。它也同样是一种琐碎而细微的协商。莱纳的角色一直拥有浓密而层次分明的思想生活，但在《转录》中，他们的思考更一贯地朝向你可以称之为“习惯化”的方向，寻找方式去适应新的氛围。适应口袋里通信与存储设备——这些设备远胜于任何后现代哲学，抹去了人在世界中具身、主观存在的那种感觉。适应子女身上的焦虑症，这些症候像先知谜语般充满不祥之意。适应小说开篇所展示的那种混杂与扭曲，即对社会—历史集体记忆的混乱转述。

paragraphs.en: In this way at least, Transcription is traditional. When fiction contends with technological change, it often situates that change in the context of generational loss. Marcel Proust’s reflection on the telephone, in the third volume of In Search of Lost Time, happens during a glitchy long-distance call with the narrator’s grandmother, in which her disembodied voice, reduced by telephony to tympanic vibration, becomes a premonition of the grave. So too in Lerner. Parents and mentors die, traces of them captured (or not) only in the fragile black box of the device. Children seem accessible only through it. The middle-generation experience, the bidirectional mourning that comes of being split between the vanishing and the emergent, preoccupies Transcription.
paragraphs.zh: 至少在这一点上，《转录》是传统的。每当小说面对技术变迁，它往往会把这种变迁置于代际断裂与失落之中。普鲁斯特在《追忆似水年华》第三卷中对电话的反思，发生在他与奶奶的一通有杂音的长途通话里：她那脱离躯体的声音，被电话传输后只剩下鼓膜振动般的声波，仿佛成了墓志般的预兆。同样地，在莱纳笔下，父母与导师会去世，他们留下的痕迹只有在脆弱的设备“黑匣子”里被保存（或未被保存）下来。孩子似乎只能透过这种装置才得以接近。站在消逝与新生之间、在双向的哀悼中撕扯着的中年体验，是《转录》的核心困境。

paragraphs.en: Read: Ben Lerner on envisioning the novel like a museum
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：莱纳：将小说想象为一座博物馆

paragraphs.en: If Lerner has an answer about how to acclimate, it’s in Transcription’s turn away from the narration of consciousness, all of those second thoughts and dreamy associations that have until now been his fiction’s primary mode. Its final section is only speech—simultaneously confessional, therapeutic, and testimonial—as if Lerner needed a form detached from the single mind. We are left just with voices, and those voices, in the novel’s subtle and canny repetitions, begin to merge with one another, becoming polyphonic. Phrases and images from earlier in the novel return, echoed, no longer assignable to particular individuals.
paragraphs.zh: 如果说勒纳对如何适应这种处境有答案，那么就在《记录》那一转：从叙事意识流式写法转向——到目前为止一贯是他小说的主要叙事方式——那些反复自问和梦幻联想被搁下。小说的最后部分只是言语叙述，兼具忏悔性、治疗性与证词性，仿佛勒纳需要一种脱离单一心智的形式。我们最终只剩下声音，而在小说巧妙而精确的重复中，这些声音开始彼此融合，成为复调。先前章节中的短语和意象回响重现，却再也不能明确归属于某个具体人物。

paragraphs.en: It’s a way of capturing the tremendous de-selving of the networked world. In this novel, one generation will depart before needing to adjust to it. One generation will never have known anything else. And one generation might be able to record its happening, to serve as the seismograph of a cognitive-technological shift. This is what one recalls of Transcription: not an action, not even really a scene, but a situation—the feeling of becoming less and less of a person. It’s a hollow feeling, but strangely also a feeling of heightened sensitivity to almost everything, including your own disappearance. In all of the novel’s layered voices, one can hear the trembling of elegy.
paragraphs.zh: 这是一种对网络世界巨大“去主体化”现象的呈现。在这部小说里，一代人将要离开这个世界，在它真正开始调整自己之前；另一代人则可能永远不会知道别的状态。还有一代人，或许能把正在发生的这一切记录下来，充当一场认知与技术转向的地震计。人们对《记录》的记忆正是如此：它不是某个动作，也不完全是一场场景，而是一种处境——越来越不再像一个“人”的感觉。这种感觉很空洞，却也奇异地带来对几乎一切的高度敏感，包括对自我消失本身的感知。在小说交叠的多重声音里，能听见挽歌的轻颤。

paragraphs.en: Early in the book, Lerner’s protagonist recalls a visit to the famous glass flowers in the Harvard Museum of Natural History: small, painfully fragile artifacts, mimicking nature with hallucinatory detail, suspended carefully from wires. He notices—or imagines; it is impossible to tell—that they are “vibrating imperceptibly.” He thinks, “The flowers were recording instruments of exquisite sensitivity.” They are like seismographs, but also like phones, like data stored in the cloud, in the air—“air looms,” Thomas jokingly puns at one point. The world, in Transcription, is becoming air: the depersonalized, dematerialized, impalpable crossing of digital signals. The glass flowers, however, suggest that art can still respond to fluctuations in, and of, the air. Lerner’s novel comes as close as we can yet imagine to honoring their delicate way of registering a change that is everywhere around us—and that is making nonsense of the difference between inside us and outside us.
paragraphs.zh: 在书的开头，勒纳的主人公回忆起一次去哈佛自然历史博物馆参观著名玻璃花的经历：这些小而脆弱得令人心疼的器物，以近乎幻觉般的细节模仿自然，悬挂在细线之上。他注意到——或者想象着注意到——它们在“悄无声息地振动”，这几乎难以分辨。他想到：“这些花是精妙敏感的记录器。”它们像地震仪，也像手机，像储存在云端的数据，也像空气——“air looms”是托马斯某处开玩笑式的双关——那种在空气中编织的“织机”。在《记录》里，世界正变成空气：数字信号所带来的去人格化、非实体化、不可触碰的流动。可这些玻璃花却在暗示，艺术仍能对空气中的起伏与变化作出回应。就我所能想象，《记录》这部小说最接近地实现了对我们四周无处不在的那种变化的细腻记录，而这种变化正在让“内在”与“外在”之间的界线变得荒谬。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “The Feeling of Becoming Less and Less of a Person.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章刊登在2026年5月纸质版上的标题是《The Feeling of Becoming Less and Less of a Person》.
